<@UEQ6M68H0>, how do you do this in Beads?

text:
Welcome - hope this helps ..

Who is your ideal early adopter? ... Why would I use Budibase, rather than say bubble or airtable. ... For people who have already looked at 10 low code apps, what is the key thing that would get them to sign up to yours?

With any website, I normally ask 2 questions. Who is this for, is it for me?.  What is the value add, what makes it better than other available options.

elements.text:
Welcome - hope this helps ..

Who is your ideal early adopter? ... Why would I use Budibase, rather than say bubble or airtable. ... For people who have already looked at 10 low code apps, what is the key thing that would get them to sign up to yours?

With any website, I normally ask 2 questions. Who is this for, is it for me?.  What is the value add, what makes it better than other available options.

text:
Hey Eddy. I love your approach. 

Who is it for:
We're targeting developers and technical entrepreneurs. 

Value add:

- We're open source, and more flexible than other platforms. With Budibase, we take away a lot of the grunt work similar to other platforms, but we also offer users the flexibility to add snippets of code within different areas of the platform (such as events) which we feel makes it more powerful. 

- We're component based, allowing users to choose from material / bootstrap components (and hopefully others in the future). 

- Users can host wherever they please. We're working on releasing the builder and we hope to add a hosting and analytics platform in the following months but we feel users should have a choice when it comes to their data. 

Budibase is closer to Bubble than Airtable. Users can build a complete Web App including their backend, frontend, and State, all within Budibase. I feel Budibase offers a better dev experience for building web apps, and I would love the opportunity to explain this more over a call if you (or anyone else) like?

elements.text:
Hey Eddy. I love your approach. 

Who is it for:
We're targeting developers and technical entrepreneurs. 

Value add:

- We're open source, and more flexible than other platforms. With Budibase, we take away a lot of the grunt work similar to other platforms, but we also offer users the flexibility to add snippets of code within different areas of the platform (such as events) which we feel makes it more powerful. 

- We're component based, allowing users to choose from material / bootstrap components (and hopefully others in the future). 

- Users can host wherever they please. We're working on releasing the builder and we hope to add a hosting and analytics platform in the following months but we feel users should have a choice when it comes to their data. 

Budibase is closer to Bubble than Airtable. Users can build a complete Web App including their backend, frontend, and State, all within Budibase. I feel Budibase offers a better dev experience for building web apps, and I would love the opportunity to explain this more over a call if you (or anyone else) like?

text: Welcome <@UTGL3V81Y>. Looking at your landing page I’d recommend working through Julian’s fantastic guide and applying it: <https://www.julian.com/guide/growth/landing-pages>. I’ve been there as well but in your current form your landing page doesn’t really reveal much to me. Everybody says they’re faster, bigger, better. Julian’s advice is to be specific.

attachments.title: Landing Page Guide: Advanced Copywriting and Design
attachments.text: Learn how to write and design advanced landing pages that compel people to convert. Featuring landing page examples and optimization best practices.

text:
Hey Martin. I love that guide. There are plans to revise the landing page . We've had 5000 signups, and surveyed 1000 of them. We are now hoping to talk with 40 this month. This research and feedback will help determine the content for the page, allowing us to focus on the crucial problems and where we add value.

But you are right, the current landing page is very run of the mill. We've no screenshots on the site which is not very insightful.

text:
I don't think privacy concerns are a major issue in post-mortem replay. Given how often people use cloud-based products where all of the customer data is in plain view of the provider's staff, people are not nearly as guarded about their data as they were in previous times.  Also, it would be elective from the user, just like Apple currently does with their crash reporter, where you are given the option to send it to apple.

In mass-market applications, the inability to reproduce client side errors is a huge problem. Adobe, MS, Apple all have millions of unsolved "cannot duplicate" issues in their bug reporting systems. In fact, the inability to fix bugs has made Jira and many other bug-tracking companies huge sums of money. You should be able to count your bugs on your hands! People are way to accepting of sloppy work in software. In Open Source there is this constant churn of changes as people fix 5 bugs and add 2 more, and with feature editions many products never stabilize (like Windows)
